SwiftKey Capstone: Exploratory Data Analysis

Miguel Rosa

2026-07-23

1. Dataset Overview

1.1 Source File Summary

The English SwiftKey dataset contains text from three sources: blogs, news articles, and tweets.

summary_dt <- train[, .(
  Lines            = .N,
  Total_Words      = sum(str_count(text, "\\S+")),
  Mean_Words_Line  = round(mean(str_count(text, "\\S+")), 1),
  Median_Words_Line = round(median(str_count(text, "\\S+")), 1),
  Mean_Chars_Line  = round(mean(nchar(text)), 1)
), by = source]

knitr::kable(summary_dt, caption = "Summary statistics by source")
Summary statistics by source
source Lines Total_Words Mean_Words_Line Median_Words_Line Mean_Chars_Line
blogs 809248 33534473 41.4 28 229.0
news 909216 30875976 34.0 31 200.8
twitter 2124133 27301754 12.9 12 68.6 
ggplot(train, aes(x = reorder(source, -table(source)[source]), fill = source)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(x = "Source", y = "Number of Lines", title = "Lines by Source") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")

Number of lines by source

1.2 Word Count Distribution

train[, word_count := str_count(text, "\\S+")]

ggplot(train, aes(x = word_count)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "#3498db", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  xlim(0, 50) +
  labs(x = "Words per Line", y = "Count", title = "Distribution of Words per Line") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(train, aes(x = word_count, fill = source)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity") +
  xlim(0, 60) +
  labs(x = "Words per Line", y = "Count", title = "Word Count Distribution by Source") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")

2. Word Frequency Analysis

2.1 Tokenize to Words

all_words <- train[, unlist(str_extract_all(tolower(text), "\\b[a-z]+\\b"))]
cat("Total word tokens:", format(length(all_words), big.mark = ","), "\n")
## Total word tokens: 92,048,596
cat("Unique word types:", format(length(unique(all_words)), big.mark = ","), "\n")
## Unique word types: 534,521

2.2 Word Frequency Table

word_freq <- as.data.table(table(all_words))
setnames(word_freq, c("word", "count"))
word_freq <- word_freq[order(-count)]
word_freq[, rank := .I]
word_freq[, freq := count / sum(count)]

cat("Top 20 most frequent words:\n")
## Top 20 most frequent words:
knitr::kable(head(word_freq, 20), caption = "Top 20 Words")
Top 20 Words
word count rank freq
the 4292707 1 0.0466352
to 2485834 2 0.0270057
and 2178441 3 0.0236662
a 2169067 4 0.0235644
of 1809153 5 0.0196543
i 1761062 6 0.0191319
in 1492012 7 0.0162090
that 998684 8 0.0108495
it 993347 9 0.0107915
for 992595 10 0.0107834
is 967414 11 0.0105098
you 921558 12 0.0100116
s 760086 13 0.0082574
on 741670 14 0.0080574
with 643305 15 0.0069888
was 562262 16 0.0061083
my 544700 17 0.0059175
at 515879 18 0.0056044
be 494564 19 0.0053729
this 490515 20 0.0053289

2.3 Zipf’s Law

ggplot(head(word_freq, 5000), aes(x = log10(rank), y = log10(freq))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.5, color = "#2c3e50") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "#e74c3c", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Log10(Rank)", y = "Log10(Frequency)",
       title = "Zipf's Law: Frequency vs Rank",
       subtitle = "Red line shows linear fit (power law)") +
  theme_minimal()

Zipf’s Law: Log-Log Plot of Word Frequency vs Rank

2.4 Word Length Distribution

word_lengths <- data.table(word = names(table(all_words)))
word_lengths[, nchar := nchar(word)]

ggplot(word_lengths, aes(x = nchar)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "#2ecc71", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(x = "Word Length (characters)", y = "Count",
       title = "Distribution of Word Lengths") +
  theme_minimal()

3. N-Gram Analysis

All n-grams are built using base R only — no extra packages.

# Sample 5% for n-gram analysis
set.seed(42)
sample_idx <- sample(nrow(train), size = floor(0.05 * nrow(train)))
sample_text <- tolower(train[sample_idx, text])

# Tokenize: split each line into words, add sentence boundary markers
tokenize_line <- function(line) {
  words <- unlist(str_extract_all(line, "\\b[a-z]+\\b"))
  c("^", words, "$")
}

cat("Tokenizing sample...\n")
## Tokenizing sample...
all_tokens <- unlist(lapply(sample_text, tokenize_line))
cat("Total tokens (incl. boundaries):", format(length(all_tokens), big.mark = ","), "\n")
## Total tokens (incl. boundaries): 4,980,658
# Build n-grams from flat token vector
# For each position, grab the preceding words as context
build_ngrams <- function(tokens, n) {
  ngrams <- character(length(tokens) - n + 1)
  for (i in seq_len(length(tokens) - n + 1)) {
    ngrams[i] <- paste(tokens[i:(i + n - 1)], collapse = " ")
  }
  ngrams
}

3.1 Bigrams (2-grams)

cat("Building bigrams...\n")
## Building bigrams...
bigrams_raw <- build_ngrams(all_tokens, 2)

# Count frequencies
bigram_dt <- as.data.table(table(bigrams_raw))
setnames(bigram_dt, c("ngram", "count"))
bigram_dt <- bigram_dt[order(-count)]
bigram_dt[, freq := count / sum(count)]

cat("Top 20 bigrams:\n")
## Top 20 bigrams:
knitr::kable(head(bigram_dt, 20), caption = "Top 20 Bigrams")
Top 20 Bigrams
ngram count freq
$ ^ 192128 0.0385748
of the 19435 0.0039021
in the 18787 0.0037720
^ i 17057 0.0034246
^ the 10707 0.0021497
to the 9516 0.0019106
for the 9092 0.0018255
on the 8963 0.0017996
i m 8391 0.0016847
it s 8089 0.0016241
to be 7309 0.0014675
at the 6363 0.0012775
and the 5685 0.0011414
don t 5646 0.0011336
in a 5483 0.0011009
with the 4742 0.0009521
is a 4595 0.0009226
it was 4368 0.0008770
for a 4205 0.0008443
and i 4128 0.0008288 
top_bg <- head(bigram_dt[!grepl("\\^|\\$", ngram)], 20)
ggplot(top_bg, aes(x = reorder(ngram, count), y = count)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#9b59b6", alpha = 0.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "Bigram", y = "Frequency", title = "Top 20 Bigrams") +
  theme_minimal()

Top 20 Bigrams

3.2 Trigrams (3-grams)

cat("Building trigrams...\n")
## Building trigrams...
trigrams_raw <- build_ngrams(all_tokens, 3)

tri_dt <- as.data.table(table(trigrams_raw))
setnames(tri_dt, c("ngram", "count"))
tri_dt <- tri_dt[order(-count)]
tri_dt[, freq := count / sum(count)]

cat("Top 20 trigrams:\n")
## Top 20 trigrams:
knitr::kable(head(tri_dt, 20), caption = "Top 20 Trigrams")

top_tri <- head(tri_dt[!grepl("\\^|\\$", ngram)], 20)
ggplot(top_tri, aes(x = reorder(ngram, count), y = count)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#e67e22", alpha = 0.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "Trigram", y = "Frequency", title = "Top 20 Trigrams") +
  theme_minimal()

Top 20 Trigrams

3.3 Quadgrams (4-grams)

cat("Building quadgrams...\n")
## Building quadgrams...
quadgrams_raw <- build_ngrams(all_tokens, 4)

quad_dt <- as.data.table(table(quadgrams_raw))
setnames(quad_dt, c("ngram", "count"))
quad_dt <- quad_dt[order(-count)]
quad_dt[, freq := count / sum(count)]

cat("Top 15 quadgrams:\n")

$ ^ i don 532 0.0001068

4. Vocabulary Coverage

4.1 How Many Words to Cover X% of Instances?

word_freq[, cum_freq := cumsum(freq)]

coverage_targets <- c(0.50, 0.75, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99)
coverage_n <- sapply(coverage_targets, function(t) {
  min(word_freq[cum_freq >= t, rank])
})

coverage_df <- data.frame(
  Target              = paste0(coverage_targets * 100, "%"),
  Unique_Words_Needed = coverage_n,
  Pct_of_Vocabulary   = round(coverage_n / nrow(word_freq) * 100, 2)
)

knitr::kable(coverage_df,
             caption = "Unique words needed to cover X% of all word instances",
             col.names = c("Coverage Target", "Unique Words Needed", "% of Vocabulary"))
Unique words needed to cover X% of all word instances
Coverage Target Unique Words Needed % of Vocabulary
50% 130 0.02
75% 1367 0.26
90% 6849 1.28
95% 16022 3.00
99% 83662 15.65 
ggplot(head(word_freq, 10000), aes(x = rank, y = cum_freq * 100)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2980b9", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 50, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 90, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  annotate("text", x = 8000, y = 52, label = "50%", hjust = 0, size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = 8000, y = 92, label = "90%", hjust = 0, size = 3) +
  labs(x = "Number of Unique Words (by frequency rank)",
       y = "Cumulative Coverage (%)",
       title = "Vocabulary Coverage Curve") +
  theme_minimal()

Cumulative Coverage by Number of Unique Words

4.2 Heaps’ Law (Vocabulary Growth)

sample_sizes <- floor(10^seq(2, log10(nrow(train)), length.out = 20))
growth <- data.table()

for (sz in sample_sizes) {
  idx <- sample(nrow(train), size = sz)
  words <- unlist(str_extract_all(tolower(train[idx, text]), "\\b[a-z]+\\b"))
  vocab <- length(unique(words))
  growth <- rbind(growth, data.table(n = sz, vocab = vocab))
}

ggplot(growth, aes(x = log10(n), y = log10(vocab))) +
  geom_point(color = "#27ae60", size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "#c0392b", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Log10(Documents)", y = "Log10(Unique Words)",
       title = "Heaps' Law: Vocabulary Growth",
       subtitle = "Red line shows linear fit in log-log space") +
  theme_minimal()

5. Profanity and Foreign Words

5.1 Profanity Check

profanity_words <- c("fuck", "shit", "ass", "bitch", "damn", "hell",
                     "crap", "dick", "piss", "bastard", "whore", "slut")

profanity_freq <- word_freq[word %in% profanity_words]

cat("Profanity words found:", nrow(profanity_freq), "\n")
## Profanity words found: 12
if (nrow(profanity_freq) > 0) {
  knitr::kable(profanity_freq, caption = "Profanity word frequencies")
}
Profanity word frequencies
word count rank freq cum_freq
shit 17899 555 0.0001945 0.6538002
hell 13445 735 0.0001461 0.6839590
ass 13130 748 0.0001426 0.6858374
damn 12739 766 0.0001384 0.6883718
fuck 12462 778 0.0001354 0.6900131
bitch 7969 1236 0.0000866 0.7392463
dick 3761 2436 0.0000409 0.8097808
crap 3729 2451 0.0000405 0.8103902
piss 996 6776 0.0000108 0.8992175
whore 752 8213 0.0000082 0.9127377
slut 562 9896 0.0000061 0.9246169
bastard 549 10032 0.0000060 0.9254373

5.2 Non-English / Foreign Words

hapax <- word_freq[count == 1]
cat("Hapax legomena (words appearing once):", nrow(hapax),
    "(", round(nrow(hapax) / nrow(word_freq) * 100, 1), "% of vocabulary)\n")
## Hapax legomena (words appearing once): 285110 ( 53.3 % of vocabulary)
foreign_heuristic <- word_freq[str_detect(word, "[^a-z]")]
cat("Words with non-standard chars:", nrow(foreign_heuristic), "\n")
## Words with non-standard chars: 0

6. Source-Specific Word Frequencies

top_words_by_source <- lapply(c("blogs", "news", "twitter"), function(src) {
  words <- unlist(str_extract_all(tolower(train[source == src, text]), "\\b[a-z]+\\b"))
  freq <- as.data.table(sort(table(words), decreasing = TRUE))
  setnames(freq, c("word", "count"))
  freq[, source := src]
  freq[, rank := .I]
  head(freq, 30)
})

all_top <- rbindlist(top_words_by_source)

ggplot(all_top, aes(x = reorder(word, -count), y = count, fill = source)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.8, position = "dodge") +
  facet_wrap(~source, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "Word", y = "Frequency", title = "Top 30 Words by Source") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")

7. Key Findings

cat("═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n")
## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
cat("KEY FINDINGS\n")
## KEY FINDINGS
cat("═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════\n\n")
## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
cat("1. DATA SIZE\n")
## 1. DATA SIZE
cat("   - Training set:", format(nrow(train), big.mark = ","), "lines\n")
##    - Training set: 3,842,597 lines
cat("   - Test set:", format(nrow(test), big.mark = ","), "lines\n\n")
##    - Test set: 426,957 lines
cat("2. VOCABULARY\n")
## 2. VOCABULARY
cat("   - Unique words:", format(nrow(word_freq), big.mark = ","), "\n")
##    - Unique words: 534,521
cat("   - Total tokens:", format(sum(word_freq$count), big.mark = ","), "\n")
##    - Total tokens: 92,048,596
cat("   - 50% coverage requires", coverage_n[1], "unique words\n")
##    - 50% coverage requires 130 unique words
cat("   - 90% coverage requires", coverage_n[3], "unique words\n\n")
##    - 90% coverage requires 6849 unique words
cat("3. TOP WORDS\n")
## 3. TOP WORDS
cat("   - Most common:", head(word_freq$word, 5), "\n")
##    - Most common: the to and a of
cat("   - Top 100 words cover", round(word_freq[100, cum_freq] * 100, 1), "% of tokens\n\n")
##    - Top 100 words cover 47 % of tokens
cat("4. N-GRAM OBSERVATIONS\n")
## 4. N-GRAM OBSERVATIONS
cat("   - Top bigram:", head(bigram_dt[!grepl("\\^|\\$", ngram)]$ngram, 1), "\n")
##    - Top bigram: of the
cat("   - Top trigram:", head(tri_dt[!grepl("\\^|\\$", ngram)]$ngram, 1), "\n")
##    - Top trigram: i don t
cat("   - Many top n-grams are stopword combinations\n\n")
##    - Many top n-grams are stopword combinations
cat("5. SOURCE DIFFERENCES\n")
## 5. SOURCE DIFFERENCES
cat("   - Twitter has shortest average lines\n")
##    - Twitter has shortest average lines
cat("   - News has most formal vocabulary\n")
##    - News has most formal vocabulary
cat("   - Blogs are most diverse in word choice\n")
##    - Blogs are most diverse in word choice

8. Plans for the Prediction Algorithm and Shiny App

This section summarizes, in plain terms, how the exploration above translates into a working product.

The algorithm. The app will guess the next word as you type, the same way a phone keyboard does. It works by looking at the last few words you typed and finding what word most commonly comes next in the training data — first checking the last 3 words, and falling back to the last 2, then the last 1, then just the most common words overall if nothing matches. This approach (called “Stupid Backoff”) is a standard, well-tested technique that is simple, fast, and doesn’t require complex statistical tuning.

Why it will work. Section 3 showed that word usage is highly skewed (Zipf’s law) — a small number of words and phrases account for most of the text, and phrase frequency drops off in a very predictable pattern as phrases get longer. That predictability is exactly what a next-word model exploits.

Managing size and speed. Sections 4–5 showed the vocabulary is large but long-tailed (hundreds of thousands of words, most used only once). Rare words will be grouped into a single “unknown” placeholder, and only the top few candidate next-words will be kept per phrase. This keeps the final model small enough to run instantly in a web browser rather than needing a large, slow lookup table.

The app. The end product will be a Shiny web app: a single text box where a user types a phrase, and the three most likely next words appear as clickable buttons — clicking one appends it to the phrase, just like a phone keyboard’s suggestion bar.

How we’ll know it’s good. Before release, the model will be tested on text it has never seen, measuring how often the actual next word appears in its top 1, 2, or 3 guesses, plus how fast each prediction takes (target: comfortably under 100ms, well below what a user would notice).

Report generated with R 4.6.1 on 2026-07-23.
